text: The Bodhisattva vow in Mahayana Buddhism — 'I will not enter final liberation until all beings are free' — is sometimes dismissed as hyperbole, but teacher Joanna Macy reads it as a functional psychological instruction. The vow deliberately suspends the ego's project of personal salvation, redirecting motivation from self-securing to world-serving. This reorientation, Macy argues, is itself the antidote to what she calls 'psychic numbing': the flatness and disconnection that sets in when we scroll through catastrophe without agency. Compassion becomes a practice not of feeling more pain but of restoring the sense that one's attention and action are causally connected to the world.
question: According to Joanna Macy, what psychological problem does the Bodhisattva vow serve as an antidote to?

options.text: Spiritual pride arising from advanced meditation practice.
The fear of death, which Macy sees as the root of all consumer behavior.
Attachment to personal liberation at the expense of community.
Psychic numbing — the flatness that arises when scrolling through catastrophe without agency.
